questions.question: Which display option is best when you need to focus on a database and need quick access to all its menus?

options.text: Inline database
options.explanation: Inline databases are designed to sit among other content, not for focused work.

options.text: Full‑page database
options.explanation: Full‑page databases occupy their own page, giving you the full menu set and minimizing distractions.

options.text: Embedded spreadsheet
options.explanation: Notion does not have a separate 'embedded spreadsheet' option; this is unrelated.

options.text: Linked database view
options.explanation: Linked views are still inline or full‑page; the key factor is the page layout, not linking.

questions.question: What is a primary visual benefit of using an inline database?

options.text: It provides a dedicated full‑screen workspace.
options.explanation: That describes a full‑page database, not inline.

options.text: It can be tucked under other pages, keeping the workspace tidy.
options.explanation: Inline databases sit within a page, allowing you to keep the overall layout compact.

options.text: It automatically creates a separate template.
options.explanation: Templates are optional and not a visual benefit of inline display.

options.text: It disables drag‑and‑drop functionality.
options.explanation: Drag‑and‑drop works for both inline and full‑page databases.

questions.question: If you want to move a database entry out of the database and turn it into a regular page, what should you do?

options.text: Select the entry and press the Delete key.
options.explanation: Deleting removes the entry; it does not convert it to a page.

options.text: Select the entry and drag it away from the database with your mouse.
options.explanation: Dragging an entry out of the database transforms it into a simple, non‑database page.

options.text: Right‑click the entry and choose 'Convert to page'.
options.explanation: There is no such context‑menu option in Notion.

options.text: Duplicate the entry and delete the original.
options.explanation: Duplicating still creates a database entry; it doesn't change its type.

questions.question: Which statement best describes a scenario where an inline database would be preferable over a full‑page database?

options.text: When you need to edit database properties in a separate window.
options.explanation: Both types edit properties within Notion; a separate window isn’t required.

options.text: When you want the database to coexist with related notes on the same page.
options.explanation: Inline databases let you place the view alongside other content, making it ideal for contextual information.

options.text: When you want to hide the database from other collaborators.
options.explanation: Visibility is controlled by page permissions, not by inline vs full‑page.

options.text: When you need to export the database as a CSV file.
options.explanation: Exporting works for both display types; it isn’t a deciding factor.

questions.question: How does dragging and dropping affect the placement of an inline database within a page?

options.text: It only works for full‑page databases, not inline ones.
options.explanation: Both inline and full‑page databases support drag‑and‑drop.

options.text: It allows you to reposition the database anywhere on the page, keeping the layout flexible.
options.explanation: Inline databases can be moved freely within the page, helping you organize content.

options.text: It converts the inline database into a full‑page database automatically.
options.explanation: Dragging only changes position; conversion requires a separate action.

options.text: It locks the database in place so it cannot be edited.
options.explanation: Dragging does not affect editability; it only changes placement.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of using a full‑page database?

options.text: Easy access to all database menus.
options.explanation: Full‑page databases indeed provide full menu access.

options.text: Ability to focus solely on the database without surrounding content.
options.explanation: The dedicated page encourages focus, which is a benefit.

options.text: Keeping the workspace tidy by nesting the database under other pages.
options.explanation: Tidiness by nesting is characteristic of inline databases, not full‑page.

options.text: Being able to view the database on its own page.
options.explanation: Viewing on its own page is precisely what full‑page databases do.

questions.question: When converting a database entry to a regular page, what happens to the original database’s structure for that entry?

options.text: The entry remains in the database as a placeholder.
options.explanation: The entry is removed from the database; no placeholder remains.

options.text: The entry is duplicated: one copy stays in the database, the other becomes a page.
options.explanation: Dragging does not duplicate; it moves the entry out.

options.text: The entry is removed from the database, and a new regular page with the same content is created.
options.explanation: Dragging out extracts the entry, turning it into a standalone page and removing it from the database.

options.text: The entry becomes a linked database view on a new page.
options.explanation: Linking is a separate feature; conversion creates a plain page, not a linked view.

questions.question: What resource does the article suggest if you need additional guidance beyond what is covered?

options.text: Visit the Notion help center.
options.explanation: The article lists 'Visit the help center' as a next step for more information.

options.text: Read the Notion API documentation.
options.explanation: API docs are not mentioned in this context.

options.text: Contact customer support via phone.
options.explanation: Phone support isn’t referenced in the article.

options.text: Join the Notion community forum.
options.explanation: While useful, the forum isn’t listed as a resource here.

questions.question: Which feature allows you to explore pre‑made layouts for databases and other pages?

options.text: The template gallery.
options.explanation: The article points to the template gallery, which hosts over 10,000 templates.

options.text: The quick find bar.
options.explanation: Quick find searches content but doesn’t provide templates.

options.text: The block menu.
options.explanation: The block menu inserts blocks; it isn’t a template repository.

options.text: The settings panel.
options.explanation: Settings adjust workspace preferences, not provide templates.

questions.question: If you need to keep a database view visible while you write a long article on the same page, which display type would likely be most efficient?

options.text: Full‑page database opened in a separate tab.
options.explanation: Switching tabs disrupts the flow of writing on the same page.

options.text: Inline database placed near the top of the article.
options.explanation: An inline view stays on the same page, letting you reference it while you write.

options.text: Convert the database entries to pages and embed them as links.
options.explanation: Linking loses the live view and defeats the purpose of quick reference.

options.text: Create a linked database view on a different page and toggle visibility.
options.explanation: A linked view on another page still requires navigation away from the article.
